q: Strong acids are strong electrolytes that completely ionise in water.
Most organic acids are classified as strong acids because they are inorganic.
Examples of strong acids include hydrochloric acid ($HCl$), nitric acid ($HNO_{3}$), and perchloric acid ($HClO_{4}$).
The basicity of an acid is defined as the number of $OH^{-}$ ions that one molecule of an acid can produce.
Weak acids are weak electrolytes that ionise only to a limited extent in water.
In common usage, the word alkali and base are strictly distinct and never interchangeable.
Sulphuric acid is a diprotic acid because each molecule can provide two $H^{+}$ ions.
Calcium hydroxide has an acidity of one because it neutralises one molecule of $HCl$.
Hydroxides of alkali metals and certain alkaline earth metals are strong bases.
Weak bases, such as ammonia and aniline, are strong electrolytes that ionise completely in water.
The acidity of a base is measured by the number of molecules of a monoprotic acid which will neutralise one molecule of the base.
A strong acid has a smaller acid ionisation constant ($K_{a}$) than a weak acid.
The larger the $K_{a}$ value of an acid, the greater its dissociation and the stronger the acid.
Small $K_{a}$ values correspond to small $pK_{a}$ values, which indicate strong acids.
Base dissociation constants ($K_{b}$) are used to determine the relative strengths of bases in aqueous solutions.
Water is included in the $K_{b}$ equilibrium expression because its concentration changes significantly with changes in other species.
The relative strengths of bases can be determined by measuring their base dissociation constants, $K_{b}$, in aqueous solutions.
Ammonia is a weaker base than the ethanoate ion ($CH_{3}COO^{-}$) and the nitrite ion ($NO_{2}^{-}$).
According to the decreasing order of acid strength, $HSO_{4}^{-}$ is stronger than $HNO_{2}$.
Small $pK_{b}$ values correspond to weak bases because they indicate low concentrations of $OH^{-}$ ions.
